maintainer_org: Browser Use (browser-use org, Zurich / San Francisco)
execution_kernel_pattern: Hybrid DOM + vision loop driving Playwright/Chromium via CDP. Each step, browser_use/dom/service.py fuses three CDP data sources — DOMSnapshot (captureSnapshot with computedStyles + paint order), DOM.getDocument(depth=-1, pierce=True), and a per-frame accessibility tree (_get_ax_tree_for_all_frames) — into a single enhanced node tree. Elements are keyed by CDP backendNodeId, filtered through visibility heuristics (display/visibility/opacity + a configurable viewport_threshold ~1000px) and ClickableElementDetector.is_interactive, plus a JS getEventListeners pass on small pages to catch click handlers. The DOMTreeSerializer then emits per-step integer indices (NOT stable across re-renders — fresh per serialization cycle, though previous_cached_state supports diffing). The LLM receives this indexed element list plus, when use_vision is enabled, the current screenshot, and must emit a Pydantic-typed action; the controller dispatches it through Playwright. This is a constrained controller loop over a fixed browser action vocabulary, not a free-form tool-call loop.

multi_agent_support.notes: Verified in source (browser_use/agent/service.py): `class Agent(Generic[Context, AgentStructuredOutput])` is single-task, single-browser_session, with no agent-to-agent messaging, role graph, planner/worker split, or shared blackboard. README shows no multi-agent example. Parallelism is achieved entirely in user code by instantiating multiple Agents (each with its own browser context) side by side.

tool_protocol: Internal action registry over Playwright (Chromium via CDP). Actions are typed Pydantic models (click_element_by_index, input_text, scroll, go_to_url, extract_content, switch_tab, done, etc.); the LLM emits a structured action object per step rather than free-form tool calls. Not MCP, not OpenAI tools — a domain-specific browser action protocol whose primary identifier is a per-step DOM index, not a stable selector.
memory_model: Short-term only in OSS. AgentHistoryList stores per-step AgentHistory items (model output, action results, BrowserStateHistory, metadata). Pruning happens at the message layer, not the history layer: MessageManager.create_state_messages() carries only the current step's screenshot, and clears self.state.read_state_images = [] each step so prior screenshots are implicitly dropped (verified browser_use/agent/message_manager/service.py). Optional _maybe_compact_messages() summarizes older text history under MessageCompactionSettings (max_history_items). TokenCost service tracks cost and injects a BUDGET WARNING above 75% of max_steps, but there is no hard token-limit enforcement. Cloud product adds persistent Profiles (cookies, localStorage) and Workspaces (file I/O); no vector store or long-term semantic memory in the library.
observability_story: OSS: step-by-step structured logs of thoughts/actions/results, every step's screenshot persisted to disk via screenshot_service.store_screenshot() under agent_directory (kept indefinitely on disk even though only the latest is sent to the LLM), optional GIF recording of the run, conversation trace dumps, and TokenCost-based cost reporting. Cloud: live preview, full session recording, webhook notifications on task completion. No first-class OpenTelemetry/LangSmith integration in the kernel, but third-party tracing wrappers exist.
typical_use_case: Driving a real browser to complete multi-step web tasks expressed in natural language — job application form-filling, e-commerce checkout (e.g. Instacart), price/spec comparison across vendor sites, scraping data from sites that require login or interaction, QA-style flow exploration, and personal-assistant browse-on-my-behalf workflows. Strong fit when the target has no API and requires login, JS rendering, or visual reasoning.
maturity_signal: mature — ~95.5k stars, 9.3k commits on main, 126 releases, MIT license, active contributor base (10.7k forks, 434 watchers), commercial entity behind it with a paid cloud, release 0.12.9 cut May 2026. Cited on third-party leaderboards (awesomeagents.ai Apr 2026 Web Agent Benchmarks Leaderboard) as 'the strongest open-source baseline' running on GPT-4o. Past the early hype phase, now consolidating an SDK API.
one_paragraph_differentiator: browser-use occupies the open-source browser-agent niche alongside Skyvern and OpenManus, and competes with OpenAI's closed Computer Use as the proprietary alternative. Unlike Skyvern, which leans heavier on a vision-first / workflow-builder product framing, browser-use is a Python library first — you import an Agent, hand it a task string and an LLM, and embed it inside larger systems. Unlike OpenManus and other general computer-use agents, it is narrowly scoped to the browser (Chromium via Playwright), which lets it ship a stable, indexed-DOM action vocabulary built from a CDP DOMSnapshot + accessibility-tree fusion instead of pixel-coordinate clicks; this trades generality for higher reliability on web tasks. Versus raw Playwright-with-prompts hacks, it provides the missing pieces: backend-node-id-keyed element indexing with shadow-DOM piercing and iframe traversal (max_iframes=100, max_iframe_depth=5), Pydantic-typed action schema, per-step screenshot rotation (only latest kept in prompt), and a multi-provider LLM adapter. Versus OpenAI Computer Use, it is open, model-agnostic, and embeddable; Computer Use is more general (any desktop app) but closed and OS-mediated.
known_anti_patterns: Cloud product surface (ChatBrowserUse, Profiles, Workspaces) is more documented than the OSS kernel internals — readers can mistake the SaaS for the library. BU Bench V1 is a self-published benchmark of 100 real-world tasks — the README shows a chart but does not publish raw numerical scores in text, and the framework has NOT published WebArena / VisualWebArena / Mind2Web numbers; the only external benchmark visibility is third-party leaderboards (e.g. awesomeagents.ai) citing it as a strong open-source baseline. Indexed-DOM element handles are fresh per serialization cycle and are NOT stable across DOM re-renders — agents that cache a click target between LLM calls will mis-click on dynamic SPAs. Single-agent design means complex workflows (planner + executor + verifier) must be assembled by the user as multiple Agent instances. Token-budget enforcement is advisory-only (BUDGET WARNING string), not a hard cap.
analysis_depth: deep — read README, docs landing, llms.txt SDK reference, plus source modules browser_use/dom/service.py (DOM tree construction pipeline), browser_use/agent/service.py (Agent loop + single-agent claim), browser_use/agent/message_manager/service.py (screenshot rotation). Cross-checked benchmark posture against third-party Web Agent Benchmarks Leaderboard (Apr 2026). DOM construction, indexing, vision token control, and single-agent design are now source-verified rather than inferred.
